title: Cult image

infobox.value: is a [human-made object](/wiki/Cultural_artifact "Cultural artifact") that is [venerated](/wiki/Venerated "Venerated") or [worshipped](/wiki/Worshipped "Worshipped") for the [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity"), [spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(supernatural_entity) "Spirit (supernatural entity)") or [daemon](/wiki/Daimon "Daimon") that it embodies or represents. In several traditions, including the ancient religions of [Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), [Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), cult images in a temple may undergo a daily routine of being washed, dressed, and having [food](/wiki/Food "Food") left for them. Processions outside the temple on special [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") are often a feature. [Religious images](/wiki/Religious_image "Religious image") cover a wider range of all types of images made with a religious purpose, subject, or connection. In many contexts "cult image" specifically means the most important image in a temple, kept in an inner space, as opposed to what may be many other images decorating the temple.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Human-made object that is venerated for the deity, person, or spirit that it represents

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/A_sculpture_of_Hindu_god_Le_dieu_Shiva_Mumbai_India.jpg/250px-A_sculpture_of_Hindu_god_Le_dieu_Shiva_Mumbai_India.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_sculpture_of_Hindu_god_Le_dieu_Shiva_Mumbai_India.jpg)

6th-century stone [murti](/wiki/Murti "Murti") of the Hindu god [Shiva](/wiki/Shiva "Shiva")

In the practice of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"), a **cult image** is a [human-made object](/wiki/Cultural_artifact "Cultural artifact") that is [venerated](/wiki/Venerated "Venerated") or [worshipped](/wiki/Worshipped "Worshipped") for the [deity](/wiki/Deity "Deity"), [spirit](/wiki/Spirit_(supernatural_entity) "Spirit (supernatural entity)") or [daemon](/wiki/Daimon "Daimon") that it embodies or represents. In several traditions, including the ancient religions of [Egypt](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt"), [Greece](/wiki/Ancient_Greece "Ancient Greece") and [Rome](/wiki/Ancient_Rome "Ancient Rome"), and [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism"), cult images in a temple may undergo a daily routine of being washed, dressed, and having [food](/wiki/Food "Food") left for them. Processions outside the temple on special [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") are often a feature. [Religious images](/wiki/Religious_image "Religious image") cover a wider range of all types of images made with a religious purpose, subject, or connection. In many contexts "cult image" specifically means the most important image in a temple, kept in an inner space, as opposed to what may be many other images decorating the temple.

The term **idol**, usually pejorative in English (except in [Indian English](/wiki/Indian_English "Indian English") where it usually carries no hostile implication), is an image or representation of a god used as an object of worship,[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) while [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry "Idolatry") is the worship of an "idol" as though it were [God](/wiki/God "God").[[4]](#cite_note-Halbertal1992p1-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Anthropologists](/wiki/Anthropologist "Anthropologist") reporting on many cultures are generally sceptical that images themselves are in fact regarded as deities within religions, rather than representations of deities; this, however, has been since [antiquity](/wiki/Ancient_history "Ancient history") the recurrent charge of those opposed to religious images.

## Ancient Near East and Egypt

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Ancient Near East and Egypt")]

The use of images in the [Ancient Near East](/wiki/Ancient_Near_East "Ancient Near East") seems typically to have been similar to that of the [ancient Egyptian religion](/wiki/Ancient_Egyptian_religion "Ancient Egyptian religion"), about which we are the best-informed. Temples housed a cult image, and there were large numbers of other images. The [ancient Hebrew religion](/wiki/Origins_of_Judaism "Origins of Judaism") was or became an exception, rejecting cult images despite developing [monotheism](/wiki/Monotheism "Monotheism"); the connection between this and the [Atenism](/wiki/Atenism "Atenism") that [Akhenaten](/wiki/Akhenaten "Akhenaten") tried to impose on Egypt has been much discussed. In the [art of Amarna](/wiki/Art_of_Amarna "Art of Amarna"), [Aten](/wiki/Aten "Aten") is represented only as the sun-disk, with rays emanating from it, sometimes ending in hands, and temples to Aten (e.g. the [Great Temple of the Aten](/wiki/Great_Temple_of_the_Aten "Great Temple of the Aten") in [Amarna](/wiki/Amarna "Amarna")) were open courts with no roof, that the Sun might be worshipped directly as it traveled across the sky.

Cult images were a common presence in ancient Egypt[[7]](#cite_note-7), and still are in modern-day [Kemetism](/wiki/Kemetism "Kemetism"). The term is often confined to the relatively small images, typically in gold, that lived in the *[naos](/wiki/Shrine "Shrine")* in the inner sanctuary of [Egyptian temples](/wiki/Egyptian_temple "Egyptian temple") dedicated to that god (except when taken on ceremonial outings, say to visit their spouse). These images usually showed the god in their sacred [barque](/wiki/Barque "Barque") or boat; none of them survive. Only the priests were allowed access to the inner sanctuary.

There was also a huge range of smaller images, many kept in the homes of ordinary people. The very large stone images around the exteriors of temples were usually representations of the [pharaoh](/wiki/Pharaoh "Pharaoh") as himself or "as" a deity, and many other images gave deities the features of the current royal family.

## Classical Greece and Rome

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classical Greece and Rome")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Athena_Parthenos_LeQuire.jpg/250px-Athena_Parthenos_LeQuire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Athena_Parthenos_LeQuire.jpg)

Recreation of the *[Athena Parthenos](/wiki/Athena_Parthenos "Athena Parthenos")* statue at the original size in the [Parthenon in Nashville](/wiki/Parthenon_(Nashville) "Parthenon (Nashville)"), Tennessee

[Ancient Greek temples](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_temple "Ancient Greek temple") and [Roman temples](/wiki/Roman_temple "Roman temple") normally contained a cult image in the [cella](/wiki/Cella "Cella"). The cella in Greek temples was in the center, while it was located in the back of Roman temples.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Access to the cella varied, but apart from the priests, at the least some of the general worshippers could access the cella some of the time, though sacrifices to the deity were normally made on altars outside in the temple precinct ([temenos](/wiki/Temenos "Temenos") in Greek). Some cult images were easy to see, and were major tourist attractions. The image normally took the form of a statue of the deity, typically roughly life-size, but in some cases many times life-size, in marble or bronze, or in the specially prestigious form of a [Chryselephantine statue](/wiki/Chryselephantine_sculpture "Chryselephantine sculpture") using ivory plaques for the visible parts of the body and gold for the clothes, around a wooden framework. Most cult statues are anthropromorphic and take human shape. The most famous Greek cult images were of this type, including the [Statue of Zeus at Olympia](/wiki/Statue_of_Zeus_at_Olympia "Statue of Zeus at Olympia"), and [Phidias](/wiki/Phidias "Phidias")'s [Athena Parthenos](/wiki/Athena_Parthenos "Athena Parthenos") in the [Parthenon](/wiki/Parthenon "Parthenon") in Athens, both colossal statues now completely lost. Fragments of two chryselephantine statues from [Delphi](/wiki/Delphi "Delphi") have been excavated.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The [acrolith](/wiki/Acrolith "Acrolith") was another composite form, this time a cost-saving one with a wooden body. A [xoanon](/wiki/Xoanon "Xoanon") was a primitive and symbolic wooden image, perhaps comparable to the Hindu [lingam](/wiki/Lingam "Lingam"); many of these were retained and revered for their antiquity. Many of the Greek statues well-known from Roman marble copies were originally temple cult images, which in some cases, such as the [Apollo Barberini](/wiki/Apollo_Barberini "Apollo Barberini"), can be credibly identified. A very few actual originals survive, for example the bronze [Piraeus Athena](/wiki/Piraeus_Athena "Piraeus Athena") (2.35 metres high, including a helmet).

In [Greek](/wiki/Greek_mythology "Greek mythology") and [Roman mythology](/wiki/Roman_mythology "Roman mythology"), a "[palladium](/wiki/Palladium_(mythology) "Palladium (mythology)")" was an image of great antiquity on which the safety of a city was said to depend, especially the wooden one that [Odysseus](/wiki/Odysseus "Odysseus") and [Diomedes](/wiki/Diomedes "Diomedes") stole from the [citadel](/wiki/Citadel "Citadel") of [Troy](/wiki/Troy "Troy") and which was later taken to [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") by [Aeneas](/wiki/Aeneas "Aeneas"). (The Roman story was related in [Virgil](/wiki/Virgil "Virgil")'s *[Aeneid](/wiki/Aeneid "Aeneid")* and other works.)

## Abrahamic religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Abrahamic religions")]

Main articles: [Aniconism in Christianity](/wiki/Aniconism_in_Christianity "Aniconism in Christianity") and [iconoclasm](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm")

Some members of [Abrahamic religions](/wiki/Abrahamic_religions "Abrahamic religions") identify cult images as idols and their worship or veneration as [idolatry](/wiki/Idolatry "Idolatry"); the worship of hollow forms, though others do not. The matter has long been controversial, depending largely on the degree of veneration or worship which is thought by opponents to be given to them. The word *idol* entered [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") in the 13th century from [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French") *idole* adapted in [Ecclesiastical Latin](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_Latin "Ecclesiastical Latin") from the Greek *eidolon* ("appearance", extended in later usage to "mental image, apparition, phantom") a diminutive of *eidos* ("form").[[10]](#cite_note-10) [Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") and the [Platonists](/wiki/Platonists "Platonists") employed the Greek word *eidos* to signify perfect immutable "[forms](/wiki/Platonic_forms "Platonic forms")".[[11]](#cite_note-11)
One can, of course, regard such an *eidos* as having a divine origin.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The [Book of Isaiah](/wiki/Book_of_Isaiah "Book of Isaiah") gave classic expression to the [paradox](/wiki/Paradox "Paradox") inherent in the worship of cult images:

> Their land also is full of idols; they worship the work of their own hands, that which their own fingers have made.

— [Isaiah 2.8](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Isaiah%202:8&version=nrsv), reflected in [Isaiah 17.8](https://bible.oremus.org/?passage=Isaiah%2017:8&version=nrsv).

### Judaism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Judaism")]

Main article: [Idolatry in Judaism](/wiki/Idolatry_in_Judaism "Idolatry in Judaism")

Judaism emphatically forbids idolatry, and considers it one of the gravest [sins](/wiki/Sin "Sin").[[14]](#cite_note-JE-14)

Judaism is [aniconic](/wiki/Aniconism "Aniconism"), meaning any physical depiction of [God](/wiki/Yahweh "Yahweh") whatsoever is disallowed; this likewise applies to cult images. The prohibition of idols within Judaism is so severe that numerous stipulations exist which are beyond simply concerning their use: Jews cannot eat anything offered to an idol as a [libation](/wiki/Libation "Libation"), cannot move openly in places where idols are present, and cannot interact with idol worshippers within certain timeframes of idolatrous festivals or gatherings.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

As time progressed and the religious traditions which the Jews were exposed to diversified, what was considered "idolatry" was subject to some debate. In the [Mishnah](/wiki/Mishnah "Mishnah") and [Talmud](/wiki/Talmud "Talmud"), idolatry is defined as worshipping a graven image through the actions of both typical idol worshippers, and through actions customarily reserved for worship of the Jewish God in the [Temple in Jerusalem](/wiki/Temple_in_Jerusalem "Temple in Jerusalem"), such as [prostrating](/wiki/Prostration#Judaism "Prostration"), [sacrificing animals](/wiki/Korban "Korban"), [offering incense](/wiki/Incense_offering "Incense offering"), or sprinkling animal blood on altars. Kissing, embracing, or "honoring" an idol, while not considered idolatry *per se*, was still forbidden.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Christianity")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Novena_a_Nuestra_Senora_del_Perpetuo_Socorro_in_Brasil.jpg/250px-Novena_a_Nuestra_Senora_del_Perpetuo_Socorro_in_Brasil.jpg)](/wiki/File:Novena_a_Nuestra_Senora_del_Perpetuo_Socorro_in_Brasil.jpg)

Brazilian [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix")

Main articles: [Religious images in Christian theology](/wiki/Religious_images_in_Christian_theology "Religious images in Christian theology"), [Byzantine Iconoclasm](/wiki/Byzantine_Iconoclasm "Byzantine Iconoclasm"), and [Protestant Reformation and art](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation_and_art "Protestant Reformation and art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Beeldenstorm_%28Iconoclastic_Fury%29_in_Antwerpen_1566_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg/250px-Beeldenstorm_%28Iconoclastic_Fury%29_in_Antwerpen_1566_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg)](/wiki/File:Beeldenstorm_(Iconoclastic_Fury)_in_Antwerpen_1566_Frans_Hogenberg.jpg)

Frans Hogenberg, *The Calvinist Iconoclastic Riot of August 20, 1566*, in [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), the key moment of the *[Beeldenstorm](/wiki/Beeldenstorm "Beeldenstorm")* in 1566, when paintings and church decorations and fittings were destroyed in several weeks of a violent iconoclastic outbreak in the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries"). Several similar episodes occurred during the early [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") period.

Christian images that are venerated are called [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon"). Christians who venerate icons make an emphatic distinction between "[veneration](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration")" and "[worship](/wiki/Worship "Worship")". [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") and [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") Christians make an exception for the [veneration](/wiki/Veneration "Veneration") of images of [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint") – they distinguish such veneration from [adoration](/wiki/Adoration "Adoration") or *[latria](/wiki/Latria "Latria")*.

The introduction of venerable images in Christianity was highly controversial for centuries, and in [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") the controversy lingered until it re-erupted in the [Byzantine Iconoclasm](/wiki/Byzantine_Iconoclasm "Byzantine Iconoclasm") of the 8th and 9th centuries. Religious [monumental sculpture](/wiki/Monumental_sculpture "Monumental sculpture") remained foreign to Orthodoxy. In the West, resistance to idolatry delayed the introduction of sculpted images for centuries until the time of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), whose placing of a life-size [crucifix](/wiki/Crucifix "Crucifix") in the [Palatine Chapel, Aachen](/wiki/Palatine_Chapel,_Aachen "Palatine Chapel, Aachen") was probably a decisive moment, leading to the widespread use of monumental [reliefs](/wiki/Relief "Relief") on churches, and later large statues. Many Christians believed that idols were not merely idle statues, but that they are inhabited by [demons](/wiki/Demon "Demon") who could exercise influence through the idol. By destroying idols, converted Christians believed to deprave devils of their earthly and material dwelling.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The *[Libri Carolini](/wiki/Libri_Carolini "Libri Carolini")*, an eighth-century work composed at the command of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne") in response to the [Second Council of Nicaea](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Nicaea "Second Council of Nicaea"), set out what remains the Catholic position on the veneration of images, giving them a similar but slightly less significant place than in Eastern Orthodoxy.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The 16th-century [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") engendered spates of destruction of images, especially in England, Scotland, Ireland, Germany, Switzerland, the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries") (the *[Beeldenstorm](/wiki/Beeldenstorm "Beeldenstorm")*), and France. Destruction of three-dimensional images was normally near-total, especially images of the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") and saints, and the [iconoclasts](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") ("image-breakers") also smashed representations of holy figures in [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass "Stained glass") windows and other imagery. Further destruction of icons, [anathema](/wiki/Anathema "Anathema") to [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans"), occurred during the [English Civil War](/wiki/English_Civil_War "English Civil War"). Less extreme transitions occurred throughout [northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe") in which formerly Catholic churches became Protestant.

Catholic regions of Europe, especially artistic centres like [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), responded to Reformation iconoclasm with a [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation") renewal of venerable imagery, though banning some of the more fanciful medieval iconographies. Veneration of the Virgin Mary flourished, in practice and in imagery, and new shrines, such as in Rome's *[Santa Maria Maggiore](/wiki/Basilica_di_Santa_Maria_Maggiore "Basilica di Santa Maria Maggiore")*, were built for [Medieval](/wiki/Medieval "Medieval") miraculous [icons](/wiki/Icon "Icon") as part of this trend.

According to the *[Catechism of the Catholic Church](/wiki/Catechism_of_the_Catholic_Church "Catechism of the Catholic Church")*:

> The Christian veneration of images is not contrary to the first commandment which proscribes idols. Indeed, "the honor rendered to an image passes to its prototype", and "whoever venerates an image venerates the person portrayed in it". The honor paid to sacred images is a "respectful veneration", not the adoration due to God alone:
>
> > Religious worship is not directed to images in themselves, considered as mere things, but under their distinctive aspect as images leading us on to God incarnate. The movement toward the image does not terminate in it as image, but tends toward that whose image it is.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Islam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Islam")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cult_image "Special:EditPage/Cult image") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Main article: [Aniconism in Islam](/wiki/Aniconism_in_Islam "Aniconism in Islam")

Towards the end of the [pre-Islamic era](/wiki/Pre-Islamic_Arabia "Pre-Islamic Arabia") in the Arabian city of [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"), an era otherwise known by the Muslims as جاهلية, or [al-Jahiliyah](/wiki/Jahiliyyah "Jahiliyyah"), the [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") or pre-Islamic merchants of Mecca controlled the sacred [Kaaba](/wiki/Kaaba "Kaaba"), thereby regulating control over it and, in turn, over the city itself. The local tribes of the [Arabian peninsula](/wiki/Arabian_peninsula "Arabian peninsula") came to this centre of commerce to place their idols in the Kaaba, in the process being charged [tithes](/wiki/Tithes "Tithes"). This helped the Meccan merchants to incur substantial wealth, as well as ensuring a fruitful atmosphere for trade and intertribal relations in relative peace.

[Muhammad](/wiki/Muhammad "Muhammad")'s preaching incurred the wrath of the pagan merchants, causing them to revolt against him. The opposition to his teachings grew so volatile that Muhammad and his followers were forced to [flee Mecca](/wiki/Hijra_(Islam) "Hijra (Islam)") to [Medina](/wiki/Medina "Medina") for protection, leading to armed conflict and triggering many battles that were won and lost, which finally culminated in the [conquest of Mecca](/wiki/Conquest_of_Mecca "Conquest of Mecca") in the year 630. In the aftermath, Muhammad did three things. Firstly, with his companions he visited the Kaaba and literally threw out the idols and destroyed them, thus removing the signs of [Jahiliyyah](/wiki/Jahiliyyah "Jahiliyyah") from the Kaaba. Secondly, he ordered the construction of a [mosque](/wiki/Mosque "Mosque") around the Kaaba, the first [Masjid al-Haram](/wiki/Masjid_al-Haram "Masjid al-Haram") of [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). Thirdly, Muhammad pardoned all those who had taken up arms against him. With the destruction of the idols and the construction of the Masjid al-Haram, a new era was ushered in, facilitating [the rise of Islam](/wiki/Spread_of_Islam "Spread of Islam").

## Indian religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Indian religions")]

### Hinduism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hinduism")]

Main article: [Murti](/wiki/Murti "Murti")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Dagdushet_Halwai_Ganpati_2005.jpg/250px-Dagdushet_Halwai_Ganpati_2005.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dagdushet_Halwai_Ganpati_2005.jpg)

A clay [Ganesha](/wiki/Ganesha "Ganesha") murti, worshipped during [Ganesh Chaturthi](/wiki/Ganesh_Chaturthi "Ganesh Chaturthi") festival, and then ritually immersed in water.

The [garbhagriha](/wiki/Garbhagriha "Garbhagriha") or inner shrine of a [Hindu temple](/wiki/Hindu_temple "Hindu temple") contains an image of the deity. This may take the form of an elaborate statue, but a symbolic [lingam](/wiki/Lingam "Lingam") is also very common, and sometimes a [yoni](/wiki/Yoni "Yoni") or other symbolic form. Normally only the priests are allowed to enter the chamber, but [Hindu temple architecture](/wiki/Hindu_temple_architecture "Hindu temple architecture") typically allows the image to be seen by worshippers in the [mandapa](/wiki/Mandapa "Mandapa") connected to it (entry to this, and the whole temple, may also be restricted in various ways).

[Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") allows for many forms of worship[[20]](#cite_note-20) and therefore it neither prescribes nor proscribes worship of images (*[murti](/wiki/Murti "Murti")*). In Hinduism, *murti*[[21]](#cite_note-21) usually means an image that expresses a Divine Spirit (*murta*). Meaning literally "embodiment", a *murti* is a representation of a divinity, made usually of stone, wood, or metal, which serves as a means through which a divinity may be worshiped.[[22]](#cite_note-kkk-22) Hindus consider a murti worthy of serving as a focus of divine worship only after the divine is invoked in it for the purpose of offering worship.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The depiction of the divinity must reflect the gestures and proportions outlined in religious tradition.

### Jainism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Jainism")]

See also: [Jain sculpture](/wiki/Jain_sculpture "Jain sculpture")

In [Jainism](/wiki/Jainism "Jainism"), the [Tirthankaras](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara") ("ford-maker") represent the true goal of all human beings.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Their qualities are worshipped by the Jains. Images depicting any of the twenty four *[Tirthankaras](/wiki/Tirthankara "Tirthankara")* are placed in the [Jain temples](/wiki/Jain_temple "Jain temple"). There is no belief that the image itself is other than a representation of the being it represents. The Tirthankaras cannot respond to such veneration, but that it can function as a meditative aid. Although most veneration takes the form of prayers, hymns and recitations, the idol is sometimes ritually bathed, and often has offerings made to it; there are eight kinds of offering representing the eight types of [karmas](/wiki/Karma_in_Jainism "Karma in Jainism") as per Jainism.[[25]](#cite_note-25) This form of reverence is not a central tenet of the faith.

### Buddhism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Buddhism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Tian_Tan_Buddha_by_Beria.jpg/250px-Tian_Tan_Buddha_by_Beria.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tian_Tan_Buddha_by_Beria.jpg)

[Tian Tan Buddha](/wiki/Tian_Tan_Buddha "Tian Tan Buddha")

Main article: [Aniconism in Buddhism](/wiki/Aniconism_in_Buddhism "Aniconism in Buddhism")

Very early Buddhism [avoided representations of the Buddha, who was represented by symbols or an empty space](/wiki/Aniconism_in_Buddhism "Aniconism in Buddhism"). Before altars became common, Buddhists would face either a Buddhist symbol or an area carved into the wall when praying or paying homage. Large images of the [Historical Buddha](/wiki/Historical_Buddha "Historical Buddha"), and [other buddhas and bodhisattvas](/wiki/Buddhas_and_bodhisattvas_in_art "Buddhas and bodhisattvas in art") later became important in many schools of [Buddhist art](/wiki/Buddhist_art "Buddhist art"), and have mostly remained so. The attitude of the devotee towards the image is highly complicated and variable in Buddhism, depending on the particular tradition, and the degree of training in Buddhist thought of the individual.

The [dharma wheel](/wiki/Dharma_wheel "Dharma wheel") is an image that used for worship in Buddhism. The Dharma represents and symbolizes all of the teachings of the Buddha. The Dharma is a wheel or circle, that maintains different qualities that are meant to be essential to the Buddhist religion. Typically, the wheel shows the eight step path that Buddhists follow to reach Nirvana. The symbol is a wheel in order to show the flow of life: Buddhists believe in reincarnation, so life moves in a circle and does not end in death.
The build of the idol depends on the school of Buddhism that you belong to.
Buddhist idols that originate from [Theravada Buddhism](/wiki/Theravada_Buddhism "Theravada Buddhism") are commonly slim, and majestic.
Buddhist idols that originate from [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism") are usually thicker, with a more dignified and nonchalant face.
Buddhist idols that originate from [Vajrayana Buddhism](/wiki/Vajrayana_Buddhism "Vajrayana Buddhism") usually have a more exaggerated posture, and usually show the Buddha / [Bodhisattva](/wiki/Bodhisattva "Bodhisattva") performing hand [Mudras](/wiki/Mudra "Mudra"). (This is common for all Buddhist Images, however the Murdras are more yoga-like)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## East Asian religions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: East Asian religions")]

### Shinto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cult_image&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Shinto")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **does not [cite](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citing_sources "Wikipedia:Citing sources") any [sources](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this section](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Cult_image "Special:EditPage/Cult image") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners"). Unsourced material may be challenged and [removed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability#Burden_of_evidence "Wikipedia:Verifiability"). *(January 2019)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

In [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto"), cult images are called [shintai](/wiki/Shintai "Shintai"). The earliest historical examples of these were natural objects such as stones, waterfalls, trees or mountains, like [Mount Fuji](/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji"), while the vast majority are man-made objects such as swords, jewels or mirrors. Rather than being representative of or part of the [kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami"), shintai are seen as repositories in which the essence of such spirits can temporarily reside to make themselves accessible for humans to worship. A ceremony called [kanjō](/wiki/Kanj%C5%8D "Kanjō") can be used to propagate the essence of a kami into another shintai, allowing the same deity to be enshrined in multiple shrines.
